author: @Kilo-nineR
text:
HowdyвА¶ what about containing that micro stove with a pot? IвАЩm interested in charging some water, sand too but IвАЩm interested in a thermal mass heater utilizing water every day at your finger tips during a day cold enough to run the heater. 

Also, what about a bearing, swivel, axle or a point to rotate on or in to oscillate? Or would that gyrate out of rhythm? 

I have a neat concept for a heater, pretty practical too.

author: @jonathanbird1534
text: Robert:- Rocket stove + old motorbike cylinder's + metal box and fan = good/cheap way to heat the  space around you I'm thinking?

author: @craigsurette3438
text:
Have you considered incorporating a thermal mass of some sort into these designs, like you see in "rocket mass heaters" to accumulate and hold the heat that is produced by the burn, and then slowly give it back to the room over time, long after the burn is finished?

That way you catch and store all those burnt calories in the thermal mass, which then does the radiant heating for you, and you  are only using the burner to charge it

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: It's amazing how we are recreating the past. Covering technologies no longer taught or utilized. Watching these videos reminded me of the technology advancement made during the time of Benjamin Franklin and the Franklin fire place. Moving the fire out of the wall and into the room. The vortex created here is very similar to the fire box on the Franklin cook stove I grew up with. Now I want some of the muffins like mom and I used to make on snowy winter days. And a pot of homemade stew on the stove top. Happy Holidays to all.

author: @lnwolf41
text: I would get some stainless steel flashing, cut slots in it. I would then make fins that have a one end bent in an L, slide it through the slots, and wrap the flashing around the top section. author: @prjndigo
text: So I'm thinking that if you add an additional ring of pipe outside the stack so that 1/3 overlaps the bottom of the vortex and the other 2/3 overlap the lower stack to gather heat from the lower stack it may produce a more stable flame.   Maybe 4" total of outer pipe open at bottom and top - should be easy to mock up with some scrap sheet metal.  Should bonus the collected pre-heated air by about 60% without increasing the temperature of the lower stack pipe.

text: So what is the advantage of a vortex flame over a normal flame in a rocket stove?

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: The advantage of the vortex is that it helps in the secondary burn of gasses given off by combusting solid/liquid fuels. Once the vortex gets going, it then increases the pull of oxygen rich air, further increasing the burn.

author: @mrintomesee
text: I did something similar. I had a tall 4inch wide square box tube from an old light that went on the decking and since i don't have a welder i just cut it bent it and riveted it together and could only find a 4inch wide length of aluminum which i wasn't expecting to last long but long enough to do an experiment (Turns out it lasted under 3 mins lol). After that i was left with the steel tube very much like what you have here. I made the pea tin with the wire mesh etc like you did and put it in just like this and was amazed the flames also shot out of the top, the only different was i didn't close the hole yet the flames still shot out of the top. I will have to try it covering the hole tomorrow. The only thing that was different was the pea tin had the mantle still on it when i placed it in the upright and the mesh was about 2 times the height of the pea tin and a little finer.

author: @wolin289
text: While you are perfecting the stove, I've been thinking of something to radiate heat long after the flame goes out.  I have these soapstone discs that go in my antique fireless cooker.  Those discs radiate heat for hours after the flame is gone.  What if you design a soapstone surround?  For those who have never seen a "Fireless cooker" work, please google.  This soapstone material is so effective that you would save a lot on fuel.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: Would soapstone work as well as Crucible ceramic Fireclay?

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 soapstone works extremely well for this application but is decidedly mor expensive than refractory clay, at least where I live in California. I have a foundry and forge both and neither are lined with soapstone.

replies.author: @wolin289
replies.text: @jerrodlopes186 I don't think you'd have to completely clad the surround in it.  Perhaps some radiating squares where the fire is hottest.  They sell tiles that aren't that expensive.  It's surprisingly efficient.  I have 3 of the soapstone discs, having bought an extra couple on ebay.  I didn't pay much for them there.  But I'm wondering how much soapstone you'd need.  1 disc  12" diameter cooks several courses of a meal and stays hot all day.  That one soapstone heats up the entire fireless cooker, and it's still hot when I remove it sometimes 8 hours later.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @wolin289 wow! You've got me intrigued. I'm going to have to get some and play with it a bit. I have a few small blocks for sculpting purposes. I suppose the discs you have would be much better for experimentation. Thank you so much for the information. Have a wonderful evening/day.

replies.author: @biscuitsbrown3209
replies.text: Soapstone is mined all over Canada and the US as well as places in S America and in N. Europe.   You can get odd pieces cheap from folks that make soapstone countertops.

Yeah exactly Nemo!
The Sand or Molten Aluminium Heat Battery can be Heated up with a Resistance,  can be Insulated with multiple Layers:
Graphite, Air , Graphite Again, Steel, Fire-brics and Aerogel.

Ceramic as Inside "Inverse Venturi" would work wonders..
Bottle Neck Shaped.. with a Secondary Turbine on ground..
So there is less weight over the top of the tower...

While the Heated Air will fall in the middle of the "Bottle-Neck" Creating Convective Fluxes with Air Heated at the Bottom...
If like in the video right here it generates a Vortex, that's Harvesting Way More Energy By Doing Technically Nothing..
Also, Since they would be already connected to Electricity anyway,
They could be use to store external Electrical Power for the night, where the Heat Difference would be more and the Convection Vortex would Increase Exponentially.

How to solve Bottle-Necks with Bottle Neck!

author: @davadoff
text:
Please explain why flame increases when door is closed. ThatвАЩs contrary to intuition. 

Please explain why you said more efficientвА¶ do you mean more effective? i.e. more heat and bigger flame by burning more fuel.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: The secret counter intuitive process at work here is the change in air pressure when you do this. Larger opening at the top of the chimney and small opening at the bottom, combined with how heat and cold air flow over the surface of the chimney itself is what causes this. Please take a close look at how giant Termite Chimney mounds work. They do the same thing naturally.

replies.author: @davadoff
replies.text:
@captainnemo6655 I know when you restrict/narrow the airflow, it accelerates. Is that why the flame gets bigger when he put the door on? Or part of the answer?

IвАЩve not looked up about termite chimneyвАЩs yet

replies.author: @davadoff
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 it seems like termite mound ventilates itself with drafts using hot air rising to pull cooler air from the bottomвА¶ but I didnвАЩt find any more specific information about what you were talking about.

author: @Kizron_Kizronson
text:
Trying to work out what the point of this is? Methanol releases a measure of heat per ml as it burns. All this design seems to do is burn the fuel faster. Sure, per minute there may be more heat, but only as a consequence of burning more fuel.
Rocket stoves on the other hand have the utility of burning gasses that were wasted in traditional wood burning designs. They have the advantage of producing more heat by combusting a secondary waste product. Not by burning more fuel.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: You gotta keep going with this line of question. The point is to improve the rocket stove by taking what nature already does inside of real life Fire Tornadoes. Mix the two approaches and you get what the point of it is? Burn the least amount of fuel initially and THEN burn the resulting gases. Two burns, one stone. Maximum heat output to be then put to use to generate thermoelectricity or mass heat.

author: @coldfinger459sub0
text:
You can make it more efficient by using the waste heat of the exhaust with coils of copper tubing running up your exhaust cold inlet goes in the bottom with gravity convection heat absorption the hot water will naturally start flowing out the top and it could go to a hot water source like a heat exchanger with a fan at some distance heating additional room.  
     We would also use it to heat our hot tub and we used it to heat our domestic hot water tank all from one heat exchanger flowing water through it off of our wood-burning stove. 

Sometimes the hot water tank for domestic water would get so hot occasionally it would blow off the high pressure high temperature valve but it was located outside the house. 

At the same time all through the winter and colder months of the year we had a nine person size hot tub in the backyard off the deck that was cap at 114¬∞F +24 seven the majority of the year all from our wood-burning stove we never had to pay for heating domestic hot water and the word came from trees that we cut down locally when we would build houses and clear lots to make room for houses. 

In this method you get the radiant heat that comes off the stove in its location locally at the point source. 

But you have all that wasted heat that goes right out the chimney and you absorb that heat and collect it in water and pump it to different locations throughout your house like I described above. 

Super easy to make fifth grade level school kid type project.

They call it The Flame Cube. Works exactly as Rob's build and yours but with a thermo electric generating twist. The thermocouples run a tiny fan that further drives the flame in the rocket stove, which means you can expand on the design by adding a vortex heating component in the chimney. Have you ever seen a cutt off diagram for Termite Chimney mounds? Please please do so, then take a look at how the ancient Romans and Greeks would heat their homes from the outside and below ground to achieve the exact same effect. It's time to get back to those roots of maximum efficiency by paying attention to how the ancients in nature and our ancestors did it.

These are thought experiments at the moment because I'm not set up to do an actual tests to determine if they would work or then work well enough to be worth adding to the system. With that in min, bear with me with the following.

Okay, so if these stoves are creating a rocket type flame...
Wouldn't at some point Newton kick in?  Are these stoves creating a downward force in addition to gravity?  One that might oscillate?

I'm thinking of the material they put in sneakers to get them to light up when up when you walk, or possibly even a larger pressure plate generator. Something along those lines.

author: @russellzauner
text:
Maybe we can make a heater that uses no added power.
Vortex tubes separate the hot and cold air molecules using air pressure differentials.
If we build a wind collector and condenser on the front of a vortex tube, perhaps we can focus enough force into it to separate the hot and cold air molecules with no added energy other than what the wind brings with it.
And in the summer we can vent the hot air and keep the cold air instead of vice versa.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text:
The Liberator Stove (previous) appears so well designed (I digested its users manual too) that I could only think of one improvident.
A viewing port for the combustion chamber would make it possible to judge the efficiency of combustion. Too much air results in too much heat going up the flue, too little and it burns rich. Industrially,  gas monitors are used but that is probably not viable for Liberator on the basis of cost. However, if the flame can be seen to be blue, then that indicates that the stove is fuel/air balanced regardless of the type of fuel used.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: I think so too, and I have a question about the construction on the insulation part of that model of burner, can it be made from Crucible Fireclay?

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text:
@captainnemo6655 This is off the top of my head. Although refractory fire-clay has low thermal expansion, expansion and contraction will need to be allowed for. Noting, that not only will the square metal section be expanding more than the clay, the temperature will change along its length. 

Perhaps forming the clay into small horizontally laid bricks and firing them before they are placed inside will suffice. 
If it was lined with one continuos layer of clay it might suffer so much cracking that the lining collapses. You could check with the clay supplier, for as you will know, different clay formulations have different characteristics.
Moulding the bricks with a Vee shaped tongue on top and a mating Vee shaped groove on the bottom will help them stack nicely without gaps for the flames to reach the steel parts.  The inside hole of the bricks could also be made round, as square inside corners lose heat and combustion may not be so good there. Hope this helps.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @Nuts-Bolts it does so much! I have actually decided on soapstone bricks and Crucible Fireclay bricks. (the kind made from termite/jungle mud). Crucible Fireclay bricks on the inside surrounded with soapstone outer facade.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @Nuts-Bolts thank you so much for your insight too! It helps more than you know :)

author: @msunites2977
text:
Greetings,

So at our camp in the woods we've been using expensive propane and I plan on making a waste oil burner.  Now we don't want the waste oil burner inside and might wish use other fuel such as wood.  We can get waste oil free basically.  I've looked at vid's how various people do such things most of which are pipe within the stack/burn chamber and use convection to heat.  Can't control the heat this way and pipe get might hot to then go inside.

So I've been considering this & wonder what you think of it.  Whether fuel source is waste oil or wood be basically a burner that heats a stock pot of water, I'm thinking 5 gallons thereabouts albeit smaller the better usage of the burn.  From there two water lines output and return, water lines run to a car radiator w/ one or two 12v box fans where can control air flow through the radiator, perhaps a 12 volt pump on the return side and/or output side.  Obviously car radiator is designed for significant flow of coolant through it so I figure simply restricting the input/output where upper/lower radiator hoses go keep hot water in the radiator longer (or perhaps transmission cooler?).

I dont want the stock pot (or turkey fryer pot) sealed as then need worry about steam pressure albeit any steam could be reused whatever, perhaps drive small electric turbine or something.  To keep water in the system a simple float valve draw water from a reserve.

Now I saw on YouTube fella who made a hot water heater out of a Propane Tank, basically burn chamber welded inside the tank isolating it and circulating water around the inside of the LPG tank, quite effective.  However, then we're talking Steam relief valves and other dangers, for example a combustion chamber failure lead to an oil water mix, water super heating, all possibilities I don't care have attended or unattended LOL.

I realize loose great of efficiency heating water, circulating it... I mean we're not talking about commercial boilers or even home boiler here which have all forms of safety features.

What might also be neat in line with you're thermal electric is using the heat generation of such a beastie to create electrical current, be real cool if it could power the pump and/or 5 volt (or 12) fan(s) on the radiator?

I've not built a lick of the concept mind you as camp is closed for the season but when get back down there it's in my reticule.  I'd love to see some videos from you exploring this concept.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: You are actually at the right place and community! Rob has an excellent video on how to make these kinds of heaters into water heaters in a prior video....also there is a product already out called "The Flame Cube" that you might want to check out. It's equal parts rocket stove, water heater and Thermo electric generator.

replies.author: @msunites2977
replies.text:
@captainnemo6655 Thanks for the reply, I did look at Roberts Rocket Stove Water Heater made w/ an LPG container.  Whilst very efficient way of doing things as I saw on another channel it's also dangerous in that superheated steam is nasty stuff.  Sure, can put pressure relief valve in place just as a common water heater has but they can fail.  When I worked in our local schools I knew the boiler mechanic's and read some of the Steam Boiler Operations books, nothing to be trifled with.  A fairly small boiler where for example fire tube failure thus water gush from the surrounding vessel into the fire tubes can result in an explosion that will readily lift a locomotive engine airborne.

The boilers I had to deal with sediment blow downs etc basically an oil burner w/ fan atomize the fuel/air mixture and ignite it down a large steel tube and in the back of the boiler there's refractory material.  That directs the heat through about 16 steel tubes back to the front of the boiler and up the exhaust stack.  Water surrounds those "fire tubes" and is turned to steam which then circulate through the building old school cast iron radiators eventually making its way back to an expansion tank.

I want something far more simple at camp whereby I can also use oil or wood firing simply by removing or changing out the heat source under a stock pot or Turkey Fryer Pot.

I've saw some vid's whereby folks coil copper tube around LPG tanks, exhaust stack etc but to me doesn't make much sense do that as why not simply apply direct heat to a vessel that holds water as a water heater does but just have it a vessel that doesn't have steam pressure.

Using Thermodynamic electric tiles as Rob has shown a cluster of those might well be able to at least spin up 5 volt, perhaps 12 volt fans behind a car radiator that'd be inside heating our place.  I doubt albeit I might be wrong that they'd produce enough current to operate pump's help move the water.   However they might be able help trickle charge car or lithium battery and then during daylight supplement the battery recharge using solar.

Key is want the "water heater" outdoors away from our living space and have it be able run unattended and safely.  Currently we'd used a Mr. Heater w/ LPG and it'll keep our place toasty.  For example 35 degree nights I'd had 68-72 degrees inside with the unit running one LPG panel which equals 8,000 BTU's.  The problem w/ LPG gas is a whole lot of heat is lost as we've intake/out vents circulate air and get rid of the carbon monoxide concerns.

With a circulating water design I don't need concern myself with that.  Atop that the Mr. Heater heat tends to go straight up, whap... So I've fans that need circulate the air or we end up with this sort of thermal layer.  Stand up to the ceiling for 5 minutes be sweating, lay down on the floor a bit chilly.

author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
text:
The Vortex has me thinking.

In a car the clean movement of hot gasses keep them away from the cylinder walls.
When it runs lean and you get pre-detonation the gases get swirled up more and as a result the combustion temperature is lower but more is transferred to the cylinder walls causing the engine to run hotter or over heat.  

If we cause a vortex in the chimney and move the gasses into the middle, the gasses circulating on the outside must therefore be cooler as the cooler air is mixing and forming the vortex. So the outside of the chimney will run cooler.

Would be a good experiment to see if this does affect the heating efficiency?

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: This principle is what makes giant termite Chimney mounds work

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: Also speaking of vortex flames, have you ever seen Fire Tornados in real life? We can use this property to completely encase it in a glass tube. My question to you is this, can the vortex flame itself be made hotter, to the point where the vortex flame heats EVEN more of those gasses released by the burning wood? In Essence a fire tornado that just keeps getting hotter and hotter?

replies.author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 it might get hotter as has it draws itself in as there is less surface mixing with colder gasses. i think the limiting factor for the heat is the fuel supply. extra heat takes extra energy, which means faster wood to gas production.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist indeed! This is my question now too... What would be the ideal energy dense fuel that we can use. Small in size put able to give off massive amounts of heat and gas, then further pyrolize the gas and so forth.... There is a chap on YouTube that built something like this, you can find his video by typing "Rocket Heater 4.0 Tornado in a tube"

replies.author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 I think the more energy dense the fuel the greater the risk there is in burning it. petrol is one to think of, lots of energy but i would not want to be burning it in the house.

author: @technicalfool
text: Seems almost like you could use the vortex generator/flue section to do both depending on current fuel availability. Clamp a wood-burning rocket stove bit on the side if you've got a bunch of old sticks. Unclamp it and put the bean tin burner head in if you've got some spare RC fuel or a bunch of old cooking oil!

replies.author: @nate_river_
replies.text: Good thinking. That really would be the ultimate burn-anything-you-throw-at-it heater.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: This idea I like and can get into. The ultimate stove. Part rocket stove and part vortex flame heater. Rocket stove part burns the solid or liquid fuels and gets the flammable gases at the right temperature and the vortex flame FURTHER combusts those gases even more.

replies.author: @rayg436
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 could even use old motor oil drip with something like this as long as it's outside

replies.author: @l0gic23
replies.text: @rayg436 or just recycle that vs putting that in your air and ground water... (Whatever goes up, comes back down).

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @l0gic23 you are correct, that's why there are always better alternative fuels that Rob showcases in his other videos, like diy methanol or diy biodeisels than can be made at home from non toxic things.

author: @Pwecko
text: Why does the flame grow stronger when you block the air supply?

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: It changes because you change the pressure differential of the air flow itself. The Vermuli Effect? Venturi Effect? I think it's called.

replies.author: @robphillips2326
replies.text: Fuel rich before, closer to stoichiometric after blocking off extra air.

replies.author: @Pwecko
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 Thanks.

author: @MerwinARTist
text: Now to make it more sexy! lol   Makes me wonder what kind of adjustments would be necessary to spin the flame more or less .. as well as what kind of fumes one is going to breathe.  I also wonder if this vortex spin system wouldn't be better working inside the new heater you got delivered.  I think a round pipe has to contribute to a better spin.  Lots to think about!  Always amazing Robert!!  Cheers mate!  рЯЩВ

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @Jeffrey Wolsieffer have you ever heard of fire proof stone paper? This material is available for making a spinning blade to help move that fire vortex around.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: I was thinking the same thing just now! And also a long the lines of that vertical spiral wind turbine blade but made out of fire proof stone paper to help move it faster under its own flow of hot air. It can be constructed upright as well as vertical with fire proof stone paper. Also why not take the cue from nature? Have you ever seen a cutt off diagram for how Termite Chimney mounds work? The secret to BOTH vortex heaters and mass heaters is right there staring right at you.

author: @toltacoatl
text: would'nt it much more effective when the vortex air-intake would only be 4-5cm high? when its as high as u did it, the sucking-effect can not be very "sharp"

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: Very good question. What size of inlet and what size of chimney have the best vortex effect.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @Jeffrey Wolsieffer good clarification. Cheers!

replies.author: @toltacoatl
replies.text: @Jeffrey Wolsieffer I believe u did not understand. the higher the vortex-generating 'zone' is, the mellower is the drive of the air coming in. we are talking about air-intake-speed ... with a huge zone like in the video it takes in a lot of air but with very little speed (air flow)

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @toltacoatl excellent point to make! And good insight

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text:
Brew alcohol, distill - first distillate (The devil's share) is methanol. Different yeast/bacteria and mash components will have different portions of ethanol and methanol.
The other way uses methane + CO2 and catalyst in water ... possibly as a byproduct of making charcoal (an added step to a wood gasification setup)

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @technosaurus3805 for this reason is why we are exploring the concepts of rocket stoves mixed with vortex heaters. Two burns, one stone.
